metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the cached pending-hint value is a `bool` or a `frozenset[str]` of pending ids: if the hint must name permanently-failing units, the cache must hold the id set (or the full `EnsureOutcome` list), not a bare boolean.
findings.finding: The plan describes the pending-hint computation as a 'cached boolean' but separately requires the hint to name permanently-failing units ('a permanently-failing unit stays out of the set and is named in the rate-limited hint'). A boolean cannot carry unit identities — there is a structural inconsistency between the cached state type and the output it must produce.
findings.location: Design section, 'Applied-set marker + write-path pending-hint' paragraph: 'pending = registry ids − parsed .ensure-applied, computed ONCE per process (cached boolean; registry static in a process)' vs. 'Accepted trade-offs: a permanently-failing unit stays out of the set and is named in the rate-limited hint'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'cached boolean' with 'cached frozenset[str] of pending unit ids (empty set = converged)' so the hint emitter can format 'N units pending: [gc-config, ...]' without a second parse of .ensure-applied.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the process-level cache holds the **pending id set** (not a scalar boolean) so the hint message can name which units are pending; update the one-liner in the Design section to read 'cached set of pending ids' (or 'cached bool derived from that set, with ids retained for hint composition').
findings.finding: The Design section describes the pending computation as 'cached boolean' yet the hint is required to name permanently-failing units ('a permanently-failing unit stays out of the set and is named in the rate-limited hint'). A boolean cannot carry the id list needed to compose that message, creating an internal contradiction an executor must silently resolve.
findings.location: Design section — 'pending = registry ids − parsed .ensure-applied, computed ONCE per process (cached boolean)' and 'Accepted trade-offs' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'computed ONCE per process (cached boolean)' with 'computed ONCE per process (cached frozenset of pending ids; truthiness used as the gate, ids used in the hint message)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update WS5's scope statement to 'In: the behavioral test suite for WS1-3 invariants (excluding fsck byte-parity/help goldens)' and add a cross-reference in WS3 AC3 noting 'behavioral no_mutate test owned by WS5 AC4'.
findings.finding: WS5's scope statement declares 'In: the behavioral test suite for WS1-2 invariants' but WS5 AC4 tests a WS3-originated behavioral requirement ('a library read-only fsck surface (no_mutate=True) mutates nothing and never runs the sweep'). WS3 AC3 asserts this guarantee but WS3's owned tests are limited to fsck byte-parity/help goldens. The scope mislabel means an implementer reading WS5's scope strictly may exclude the no_mutate test as 'WS3 territory', leaving WS3's behavioral no_mutate guarantee with no behavioral test owner.
findings.location: child f9b6-744e-636b-4430 (WS5) scope statement and AC4; child 424c-c66a-a5c0-49ab (WS3) AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Change WS5 scope 'In' line to read 'the behavioral test suite for WS1-3 invariants (excluding fsck byte-parity/help goldens, which are owned by WS3)'. Add a parenthetical to WS3 AC3: '(behavioral test for this invariant is WS5 AC4)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the lock-acquisition policy for MCP startup explicitly — e.g. 'attempt non-blocking acquire; if lock is unavailable, log at DEBUG and skip the sweep (best-effort)' — so an executor does not have to choose.
findings.finding: The plan says MCP server startup runs run_ensures 'under write lock' and 'best-effort — log-and-continue, never abort boot', but does not specify what to do when the write lock is already held at the moment of startup (wait indefinitely? timeout-and-skip? skip-immediately?). An executor cannot implement lock acquisition at MCP boot without choosing a timeout/skip policy.
findings.location: Design section — 'Entry points (non-hot): … MCP server startup (best-effort — log-and-continue, never abort boot; under write lock)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the MCP startup entry-point description: 'non-blocking lock acquire; if unavailable, log at DEBUG level and skip (never block MCP boot)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reword AC1 to reflect that the registry covers units appearing on BOTH the re-init path (init.py:209-213) and the fresh-init path (init.py:242-248), or explicitly note 'these same units are removed from both paths per AC3', so the label 're-init units' does not imply a narrower scope.
findings.finding: AC1 labels the migrated units 'the 5 re-init units' but the Design body explicitly states these same units 'also appear on the fresh-init path (init.py:242-248)' and AC3 states the migration removes hand-listed calls from 'BOTH re-init and fresh-init paths'. An implementer reading AC1 in isolation may interpret scope as only the re-init path (209-213), missing the fresh-init removal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 1 ('the 5 re-init units'); Design body ('the exact 5 re-init idempotent steps migrated (init.py:209-213)'); AC3 ('removes the hand-listed calls from BOTH re-init and fresh-init paths')
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC1's opening phrase from 'generalizes the 5 re-init units' to 'generalizes the 5 units present on both the re-init path (init.py:209-213) and the fresh-init path (init.py:242-248)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate exactly one child as owner of the `run_ensures()` idempotency git-log test: either keep it in WS1 (unit-level, immediate) and remove it from WS5 AC1, OR keep it in WS5 (behavioral suite) and relax WS1 AC3 to 'check-then-act returns ok/no-op status' (observable at the EnsureOutcome level, not git-log level).
findings.finding: WS1 AC3 and WS5 AC1 both specify the same observable test: '`run_ensures()` twice on a converged store makes zero changes and zero git commits (git-log assertion)'. The test is owned by two separate children with no deduplication note, creating a scope overlap that risks either duplication (two near-identical tests that diverge over time) or a gap (each implementer assumes the other wrote it).
findings.location: child ec29-af41-884e-420d (WS1) AC3 and child f9b6-744e-636b-4430 (WS5) AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the git-log idempotency assertion exclusively to WS5 AC1 (behavioral suite). Reword WS1 AC3 to verify the unit-level observable: 'a second `run_ensures()` on a converged store returns all-ok EnsureOutcomes and the callable is confirmed to tree-check before committing (no commit API called)' — testable without a real git repo in WS1's unit tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly characterize the existing error contract of the 5 migrated init functions (do any raise on failure? do callers rely on that?), document the skip-and-continue behavioral change as a justified trade-off, and add a back-out path (e.g., re-init or a specific repair step) for the case where a unit silently fails during init.
findings.finding: The plan changes init-path error handling from individually-called functions (whose failure contract — raise vs. swallow — is not characterized) to skip-and-continue under `run_ensures()`, but does not explicitly acknowledge whether callers of init currently rely on these functions raising on failure, nor provide a back-out mechanism if a unit silently skips during init and leaves a store misconfigured.
findings.location: Design section: 'run_ensures() … catches a raising unit (skip-and-continue → failed)' and AC: 'skip-and-continue; init logs failures, never aborts'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to the Design (or to `docs/migrations.md`) that states: (a) the existing functions are best-effort / do not have strong failure contracts [verify this], (b) skip-and-continue is explicitly preferred over fail-loudly at init to preserve store usability, (c) a unit that persistently fails will appear in the rate-limited hint and can be remediated via `rebar fsck --repair`. If any migrated function currently raises in a way callers depend on, use an expand-contract sequence: keep the old call site raising for one release, then remove it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'the `[ensure]` config section (`hint_interval_secs`, `hint_enabled`), their defaults, and how operators use them to tune or suppress hints' to the required content of `docs/migrations.md`.
findings.finding: The new `[ensure]` config section (`ensure.hint_interval_secs`, `ensure.hint_enabled`) is user-facing configuration that operators need to suppress noisy hints or tune the interval, but the required content list for `docs/migrations.md` in the AC does not include documenting these config knobs or how users interact with the `[ensure]` section.
findings.location: AC line: 'docs/migrations.md documents reuse, marker-gates-hint-never-repair, hot-path contract, long-lived-MCP trade-off, post-rollback file-ignore, and A-tier future; gc-config is the reference unit'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC doc requirement: '… and the `[ensure]` config section (`ensure.hint_interval_secs` default 86400, `ensure.hint_enabled` default true) with operator guidance for suppression in long-lived environments.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to `docs/migrations.md` AC: document the hook-inclusion rule (event-boundary paths via `_seam`/`event_append`) vs. intentionally-excluded inline-committers (`compact.py`, `txn.py`, `delete.py`), so future contributors have a written rule for new mutation paths.
findings.finding: The `docs/migrations.md` AC does not include the rationale for intentionally excluding the inline-committer paths (`compact.py`, `txn.py`/`transition_close.py`, `delete.py`) from the pending-hint hook, leaving future maintainers without documented guidance on when to add or skip hook wiring for new mutation paths.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last AC item (docs/migrations.md) and Design section 'Best-effort write-path pending-hint'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the last AC item to read: '…gc-config is the reference unit; **documents the hook-inclusion rule (event-boundary paths) vs. intentionally-excluded inline-committers and the acceptance rationale (rate-limited nudge, not correctness)**'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact MCP startup injection point for `run_ensures` (e.g., a preamble call in `main()` before `build_server().run()`, or a FastMCP lifespan context), and confirm it runs before the server accepts connections while honoring the 'never abort boot' contract.
findings.finding: The MCP server startup injection point for `run_ensures` is unspecified. `mcp_server.py:main()` and `build_server()` have no existing hook to the store or init layer; FastMCP's `run()` provides no documented lifespan callback in the codebase. Without naming the specific injection point (e.g., a `lifespan` async context, a lazy first-tool-call hook, or a `main()` preamble before `build_server().run()`), the implementer cannot derive where to place the best-effort `run_ensures` call.
findings.location: Design section — 'Entry points (non-hot)' / 'MCP server startup (best-effort — log-and-continue, never abort boot; under write lock)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Design section: 'In `mcp_server.main()`, call `run_ensures(tracker)` in a try/except immediately before `build_server().run()` — after the stderr handler is installed, before the stdio transport starts. This placement is always reached on startup, runs outside any per-request timeout, and a raised exception is caught-and-logged without blocking the server from starting.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the Design section specifying that on the fresh-init path, `run_ensures` (and its store write lock acquisition) must be called AFTER the tracker directory is created, and that this nesting (init-mkdir-lock outer, store-write-lock inner) is safe because the two locks target distinct file paths.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `run_ensures()` runs 'under the store write lock at all init entry points' but does not address the lock-nesting on the fresh-init path, where the existing `_acquire_init_lock` (the `ticket-init.lock` mkdir lock) is already held when `run_ensures` would acquire the store write lock. While the two locks target different files and no deadlock results, the implementer needs to know: (a) on the fresh-init path, `run_ensures` must be called AFTER the tracker directory is created (since `.ticket-write.lock` lives inside it), and (b) this creates a nested-lock region that is not currently documented in `lock.py`'s invariant commentary.
findings.location: Design section — 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock' / AC: 'run_ensures() runs unconditionally under the store write lock at all init entry points'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback-state assertion to the plan (and AC or docs/migrations.md) that explicitly covers the committed tickets-branch tree state after a code rollback: confirm the .gitignore/.gitattributes content added by ensure units is strictly additive and backward-compatible with pre-migration rebar, and that a partially-applied sweep (some committing units done, others not) is safe under both old and new binaries.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback safety claim covers only the two git-ignored marker files (.ensure-applied, .ensure-hinted), but two ensure units (gitattributes, gitignore) make permanent git commits to the tickets branch. The plan never asserts the post-rollback state of the tickets branch tree — specifically, whether the new .gitignore/.gitattributes entries committed by ensure units are backward-compatible with pre-migration rebar code, and whether a partial run (only one of the two committing units having committed) leaves the store in a correct intermediate state under an older binary.
findings.location: Accepted trade-offs / Design: 'Post-rollback: old code simply ignores the git-ignored `.ensure-applied`/`.ensure-hinted` files (no schema coupling).'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design's 'Accepted trade-offs' section and in docs/migrations.md, add: 'Post-rollback committed-tree state: the gitignore and gitattributes ensure units add only new entries (additive). Old rebar code that re-runs _commit_gitignore/_commit_gitattributes will find the files already present (show.returncode==0) and apply its own missing-lines check idempotently — the new entries are invisible to old missing-lines logic and are not stripped. No intermediate partial state (only one of two committing units done) is harmful under either binary.' If this assertion cannot be verified, add it as an explicit AC test: run ensure sweep, kill after first committing unit, roll back binary, run old init.py re-init path, assert no new spurious commits or entry deletions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit AC or design note specifying that `EnsureConfig` integration requires additions to `Config` dataclass field, `_SECTION_CLASSES['ensure']`, and `_SECTIONS['ensure']` (with `_as_int(minimum=1)` for `hint_interval_secs` and `_as_bool` for `hint_enabled`) in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan references a new `EnsureConfig` dataclass to be added to `_config_schema.py` with `ensure.hint_interval_secs=86400` and `ensure.hint_enabled=true`, but does not specify the required coercion table entries (`_SECTIONS['ensure']`, `_SECTION_CLASSES['ensure']`, and a field on `Config`) nor the type coercers for each key. The plan's 'no edits to any generated file' language implies the wiring is trivial, but `_config_schema.py` is hand-maintained and all three tables must be extended for the new section to be loadable.
findings.location: Design section, 'Applied-set marker + write-path pending-hint': 'Typed config (EnsureConfig in _config_schema.py: ensure.hint_interval_secs=86400, ensure.hint_enabled=true)'; AC7: 'typed-config interval (86400s) + [ensure] config-section suppression'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (e.g., WS1) as sole owner of the `_GITIGNORE` constant edit and add both `.ensure-applied` and `.ensure-hinted` there; WS2 must depend on / sequence after WS1 for this artifact and must not independently re-edit the same list.
findings.finding: WS1 (ec29) and WS2 (891c) both independently modify the same `_GITIGNORE` constant (init.py:38-48) — WS1 adds `.ensure-applied` and WS2 adds `.ensure-hinted` — with no designated owner and no coordination mechanism stated. If implemented as separate PRs/branches, the second merge may overwrite the first entry, violating parent AC8 which requires BOTH files to be git-ignored.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign `_GITIGNORE` (init.py:38-48) ownership to WS1, which already adds `.ensure-applied`. Update WS1 What/AC to also add `.ensure-hinted` (placeholder entry, with a note that WS2 defines its semantics), and remove the `_GITIGNORE`-edit claim from WS2's scope. Alternatively, add an integration-test assertion in WS5 that the `_GITIGNORE` constant contains BOTH entries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or explicit test requirement confirming that a pre-migration rebar version operating on a store containing `.ensure-applied` and `.ensure-hinted` produces no error and no behavior change (validates the stated rollback safety claim).
findings.finding: The rollback safety claim ('old code simply ignores the git-ignored `.ensure-applied`/`.ensure-hinted` files') is asserted in prose but no AC or test requirement validates this behavior. If either file's format or presence causes an unexpected interaction with an older version, the rollback path is untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A store containing `.ensure-applied` and `.ensure-hinted` operated on by a pre-migration rebar version produces no error and no changed behavior (files are git-ignored; no legacy code path reads them — verify by grep/audit of old read paths).' This can be a code-review / audit AC rather than a runtime test if a search confirms no legacy reader.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or specify) the exact mechanism by which `ensure.hint_interval_secs` and `ensure.hint_enabled` are exposed as env-var overrides; if rebar has no generic env-to-config-key mapping, the plan must describe the explicit lookup code needed (e.g. `REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_INTERVAL_SECS` checked in the hint path) rather than asserting 'auto-derived'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the env override is auto-derived per rebar's standard mechanism — no edits to any generated file' for the new [ensure] config section (ensure.hint_interval_secs, ensure.hint_enabled). Codebase inspection shows no generic env-var auto-derive for config section keys: every existing env-gated config value (e.g. REBAR_MCP_READONLY → mcp.readonly) requires explicit lookup code written in a consumer module (mcp_server._readonly → rebar.config.mcp_readonly()). _config_schema.py's _SECTIONS/_SECTION_CLASSES tables handle TOML parsing only, not env-var resolution. The assertion that env overrides are 'auto-derived' is therefore unsupported by codebase evidence and may require writing explicit env-var lookup code for the two new keys.
findings.location: Design section: 'Interval + suppression live in the [ensure] config section (ensure.hint_interval_secs=86400, ensure.hint_enabled=true); the env override is auto-derived per rebar's standard mechanism — no edits to any generated file.'
findings.suggested_fix: Audit config.py for the env-var resolution path used by other sections; if it is not generic (current evidence says it is not), add an explicit note in the plan: 'EnsureConfig values are read from TOML only; env overrides require explicit lookup in the hint path: REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_INTERVAL_SECS and REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_ENABLED, read by the same helper used for REBAR_MCP_READONLY.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
